docs.title: Data Import
docs.text: ApexBase is designed to move data in and out of Python data tools without turning every workflow into a custom ETL job.

docs.title: Choose An Import Path
docs.text: Source Best method Python records client.store(dict) or client.store(list[dict]) Large Python batches client.store(columnar_dict) Pandas client.from_pandas(df, table_name=...) Polars client.from_polars(df, table_name=...) PyArrow client.from_pyarrow(table, table_name=...) CSV / JSON / Parquet one-off query SQL read_csv(), read_json(), read_parquet() CSV / JSON / Parquet repeated queries register_temp_table()

docs.title: Columnar Batches
docs.text:
Columnar dictionaries are usually the fastest pure-Python ingest format:

client.create_table("events")
client.store({
    "user_id": [1, 2, 3],
    "event": ["signup", "click", "purchase"],
    "value": [0.0, 0.0, 29.9],
})

docs.title: DataFrames
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl
import pyarrow as pa

client.from_pandas(
    pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Alice"], "age": [30]}),
    table_name="users",
)

client.from_polars(
    pl.DataFrame({"name": ["Bob"], "age": [25]}),
    table_name="users",
)

client.from_pyarrow(
    pa.table({"name": ["Charlie"], "age": [35]}),
    table_name="users",
)

When table_name is provided, ApexBase selects or creates that table for the import.

docs.title: Query Files Directly
docs.text:
result = client.execute("""
    SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS rows
    FROM read_csv('events.csv')
    GROUP BY city
    ORDER BY rows DESC
""")

client.execute("SELECT * FROM read_parquet('orders.parquet') LIMIT 10")
client.execute("SELECT * FROM read_json('events.ndjson') WHERE kind = 'click'")

Direct file functions are a good fit for ad hoc analysis and one-time joins.

docs.title: Register Temporary Tables
docs.text:
For repeated queries against the same file, parse it once and use a temporary table:

client.register_temp_table("events_file", "events.csv")

client.execute("""
    SELECT event, COUNT(*) AS rows
    FROM events_file
    GROUP BY event
""")

client.drop_temp_table("events_file")

Temporary tables are cleaned up when the client closes.

docs.title: Join Files With Stored Data
docs.text:
client.execute("""
    SELECT u.name, f.event
    FROM users u
    JOIN read_csv('events.csv') f ON u.id = f.user_id
    WHERE f.event = 'purchase'
""")

docs.title: Parquet Interop
docs.text:
client.execute("COPY users TO 'users.parquet' (FORMAT PARQUET)")
client.execute("COPY users FROM 'users.parquet' (FORMAT PARQUET)")

docs.title: Performance Tips
docs.text:
• Create tables with schemas for large repeated imports.
  • Prefer columnar dictionaries or DataFrame import for bulk writes.
  • Use register_temp_table() when querying the same file many times.
  • Convert results to Arrow or Polars when downstream code is columnar.
  • Use durability="fast" for scratch imports and durability="safe" or durability="max" for application data.

docs.title: Python Client Guide
docs.text: The Python client is the primary ApexBase interface for embedded applications, scripts, and notebooks.

docs.title: Client Lifecycle
docs.text:
Use a context manager so files and temporary tables are cleaned up reliably:

from apexbase import ApexClient

with ApexClient("./data", durability="safe") as client:
    client.create_table("events")
    client.store({"kind": "signup", "user_id": 1})

For repeatable examples or tests, start from a clean directory:

with ApexClient.create_clean("./tmp_data") as client:
    client.create_table("scratch")

docs.title: Database And Table Selection
docs.text:
client.use_database("analytics")
client.create_table("events")
client.use_table("events")

client.use(database="analytics", table="events")

use(database=..., table=...) switches database context and creates the table if it is missing.

docs.title: Writing Data
docs.text:
Single row:

client.store({"name": "Alice", "age": 30})

Multiple rows:

client.store([
    {"name": "Bob", "age": 25},
    {"name": "Charlie", "age": 35},
])

Columnar batch:

client.store({
    "name": ["Diana", "Eve", "Frank"],
    "age": [29, 31, 44],
})

For large inserts, prefer columnar batches or DataFrame import.

docs.title: Querying
docs.text:
Use execute() for SQL:

result = client.execute("""
    SELECT age, COUNT(*) AS users
    FROM users
    GROUP BY age
    ORDER BY users DESC
""")

Use query() for simple table-scoped filtering:

result = client.query(where_clause="age >= 30", limit=100)

Use record helpers when you already know row ids:

row = client.retrieve(1)
rows = client.retrieve_many([1, 2, 3])
all_rows = client.retrieve_all()

docs.title: Working With Results
docs.text:
result = client.execute("SELECT * FROM users")

print(result.shape)
print(result.columns)

rows = result.to_dict()
df = result.to_pandas()
pl_df = result.to_polars()
arrow_table = result.to_arrow()

ResultView is intentionally lightweight: keep it while you need query results, then convert to the format your application already uses.

docs.title: Updating And Deleting
docs.text:
client.replace(1, {"name": "Alice", "age": 31})
client.batch_replace({
    2: {"name": "Bob", "age": 26},
    3: {"name": "Charlie", "age": 36},
})

client.delete(where_clause="age < 18")

SQL DML is also supported:

client.execute("UPDATE users SET age = 31 WHERE name = 'Alice'")
client.execute("DELETE FROM users WHERE age < 18")

docs.title: Schema Changes
docs.text:
client.add_column("email", "String")
client.rename_column("email", "contact_email")
client.drop_column("contact_email")
print(client.list_fields())

For SQL-first workflows:

client.execute("ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email STRING")

docs.title: Durability And Flushing
docs.text:
client = ApexClient("./data", durability="safe")
client.flush()

Use fast for maximum throughput, safe for balanced persistence, and max when every write must be fsynced.

docs.title: Where To Go Next
docs.text:
• SQL Guide for supported query patterns.
  • Data Import for DataFrame and file workflows.
  • Python API Reference for complete method signatures.

docs.title: Server Protocols
docs.text: ApexBase is embedded by default, but it can also expose the same data over PostgreSQL Wire and Arrow Flight.

docs.title: Which Protocol Should I Use?
docs.text: Interface Best for Command Python API Embedded apps, scripts, notebooks ApexClient("./data") PostgreSQL Wire DBeaver, DataGrip, psql, pgAdmin, BI tools, libpq clients apexbase-server Arrow Flight Fast columnar transfer into Arrow-native systems apexbase-flight Combined server Running both protocols from one process apexbase-serve

docs.title: Start Both Servers
docs.text:
apexbase-serve --dir ./data

Default ports:

Protocol Port PostgreSQL Wire 5432 Arrow Flight 50051

Custom ports:

apexbase-serve --dir ./data --pg-port 15432 --flight-port 50052

Disable one protocol:

apexbase-serve --dir ./data --no-flight
apexbase-serve --dir ./data --no-pg

docs.title: PostgreSQL Wire
docs.text:
Start only the PostgreSQL-compatible server:

apexbase-server --dir ./data --host 127.0.0.1 --port 5432

Connect with psql:

psql -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5432 -d apexbase

Connect with Python:

import psycopg2

conn = psycopg2.connect(host="127.0.0.1", port=5432, dbname="apexbase")
cur = conn.cursor()
cur.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users")
print(cur.fetchall())

Authentication is currently disabled for the local server; any credentials supplied by clients are accepted.

docs.title: Arrow Flight
docs.text:
Start only the Flight server:

apexbase-flight --dir ./data --host 127.0.0.1 --port 50051

Query with PyArrow Flight:

import pyarrow.flight as fl

client = fl.connect("grpc://127.0.0.1:50051")
table = client.do_get(fl.Ticket(b"SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 100")).read_all()
df = table.to_pandas()

Arrow Flight is the best option when result sets are large and the consumer can work with Arrow tables.

docs.title: Operational Notes
docs.text:
• Use the same --dir that your embedded Python or Rust app uses.
  • Run one writer-heavy workload at a time unless your application has tested its concurrency pattern.
  • Prefer binding to 127.0.0.1 for local tools.
  • Put the server behind your own network controls if exposing it beyond the machine.

docs.title: SQL Guide
docs.text: ApexBase supports a practical SQL dialect for embedded HTAP workloads: DDL, DML, analytical SELECT queries, joins, CTEs, set operations, transactions, table functions, full-text search, and vector search.

docs.title: Running SQL
docs.text:
from apexbase import ApexClient

with ApexClient("./data") as client:
    client.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users")
    client.execute("INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 30)")
    result = client.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE age >= 18")

docs.title: DDL
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users;
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN name STRING;
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN age INT;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS old_users;

Use qualified names for named databases:

CREATE TABLE analytics.events;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS analytics.old_events;

docs.title: DML
docs.text:
INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 30);
INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ('Bob', 25), ('Charlie', 35);

UPDATE users SET age = 31 WHERE name = 'Alice';
DELETE FROM users WHERE age < 18;

docs.title: Analytical Queries
docs.text:
SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS users, AVG(age) AS avg_age
FROM users
WHERE age BETWEEN 18 AND 65
GROUP BY city
HAVING users > 10
ORDER BY avg_age DESC
LIMIT 20;

docs.title: Joins
docs.text:
SELECT u.name, e.event, e.ts
FROM users u
JOIN events e ON u.id = e.user_id
WHERE e.event = 'purchase';

Supported join forms include INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, and CROSS.

docs.title: CTEs And Subqueries
docs.text:
WITH active_users AS (
    SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS events
    FROM events
    GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT u.name, a.events
FROM users u
JOIN active_users a ON u.id = a.user_id
WHERE a.events > 5;

SELECT *
FROM users
WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM events WHERE event = 'signup');

docs.title: Window Functions
docs.text:
SELECT
    user_id,
    ts,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS event_rank,
    COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id) AS user_events
FROM events;

docs.title: Set Operations
docs.text:
SELECT user_id FROM web_events
UNION
SELECT user_id FROM mobile_events;

SELECT user_id FROM paid_users
EXCEPT
SELECT user_id FROM refunded_users;

UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, and EXCEPT are supported.

docs.title: Transactions
docs.text:
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO orders (order_id, total) VALUES (1001, 39.9);
SAVEPOINT before_adjustment;
UPDATE orders SET total = 35.9 WHERE order_id = 1001;
RELEASE before_adjustment;
COMMIT;

Use ROLLBACK to cancel a transaction, or ROLLBACK TO savepoint_name to undo part of one.

docs.title: File Table Functions
docs.text:
Query files directly:

SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS rows
FROM read_csv('events.csv')
GROUP BY city;

Supported functions:

  • read_csv(path)
  • read_parquet(path)
  • read_json(path)

For repeated queries over a file, register it as a temporary table from Python. See Data Import.

docs.title: Full-Text Search
docs.text:
CREATE FTS INDEX ON articles(title, content);

SELECT title
FROM articles
WHERE MATCH('rust database');

For fuzzy matching, lifecycle commands, and configuration, see the Full-Text Search Guide.

docs.title: Vector Search
docs.text:
SELECT *
FROM explode_rename(
    topk_distance('items', 'embedding', '[0.1, 0.2, 0.3]', 10, 'cosine'),
    '_id,dist'
);

For Python batch search and float16 vector storage, see the Float16 Vector Guide.

docs.title: Explain
docs.text:
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 30;
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT city, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY city;

Use EXPLAIN when you are checking whether a query is taking a fast path, using an index, or falling back to the full planner.
